context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m stuck on this reflection about the informal economy and illegality. The prompt feels really broad, and I can’t tell if I’m supposed to explain causes, debate formalization, or focus on who actually does the planning.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair observation — the prompt does cover a lot of ground. But before we talk structure, let me ask you this: when you think about informal settlements in developing countries, what's your instinct about *why* they exist in the first place? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling overwhelmed by the breadth of the prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them or scaffolding every sub-question, I should help them identify what they already know and let them take ownership of the direction. A single orienting question will help them self-organize without me doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: Honestly, my instinct is just “because people are poor and the state can’t keep up,” but that feels too simplistic. I’m not sure if that’s an okay starting point or if I’m already missing the bigger theory angle.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a reasonable starting point — but let me push on it a little. You said the state 'can't keep up.' Does that assume the state is *trying* to keep up? What if informality isn't just a failure of capacity, but relates to something about how the state and poor people relate to each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a decent intuition but is second-guessing themselves. Rather than validating or correcting directly, I should push them to interrogate their own reasoning further. The 'state can't keep up' framing is worth probing — it assumes the state is trying to keep up, which is itself a debatable premise. A pointed question will help them discover a more nuanced angle on their own.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s where I get fuzzy. I can sort of see that informality might be produced by the state too, not just ignored by it, but then I lose the thread and want someone to just explain the whole picture plainly.
exchanges.tutor: You're onto something important there — hold that thread. If the state can *produce* informality, what might motivate it to do so? Think about who benefits when large numbers of poor people remain in an informal, legally ambiguous situation.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting closer to a key insight — that the state can actively produce informality, not just fail to prevent it. This is a significant conceptual move. Rather than explaining the whole picture, I should affirm the direction and ask them to develop this specific idea further. They need to work through it themselves.

exchanges.student: I guess elites or employers might benefit because informal labor is cheaper and easier to control, and the state might benefit from selective enforcement. But I still feel like I need a big overview of the entire prompt, because I’m piecing together fragments and not seeing how they connect.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation about selective enforcement — that's actually a key idea worth developing in your paper. As for the 'big overview,' I'd push back: you already have several connected pieces. You have *why* informality exists, *who produces it*, and *who benefits*. What do you think the relationship looks like between that informal economy you're describing and the formal one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good analytical progress but is asking for a comprehensive overview, which would undermine their own reasoning process. I should acknowledge the good insight and redirect them to start connecting their own fragments rather than waiting for me to synthesize it for them. I'll point them toward the relationship between informality and the formal economy, which is the next logical thread.

exchanges.student: I keep wanting to say they’re separate, but I know that’s probably wrong. That didn’t really help me organize it, so maybe I’m trying the wrong angle — could you just give me a simple overall argument I could react to, because I’m not sure what position this paper is even asking me to take?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you an argument to react to — that's your job, and you're more ready than you think. But let's stay with your instinct: you said informal and formal are probably *not* separate. What makes you say that? Give me one concrete example of how they might be connected.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide an argument for them, which I must refuse. However, they're also showing some frustration. I should be direct about my role, but also help them see that they're closer than they think. The 'separate but wrong' instinct is worth pressing — it's the right thread to pull on, and connecting informal/formal economies is central to the prompt. I'll redirect firmly but constructively.

exchanges.student: Maybe formal businesses rely on informal workers, suppliers, or settlements even while pretending they don’t. I’m still not seeing the full map, though, and I think I need the prompt broken into chunks or I’m going to keep circling.
exchanges.tutor: That example about formal businesses relying on informal workers is exactly the kind of insight your paper needs. Look — you've now built three solid ideas on your own: informality is partly state-produced, it benefits certain actors, and informal/formal economies are intertwined. That's already a reflection. Rather than mapping everything before writing, why not try drafting a paragraph or two using what you have? What happens when you put those three ideas together in writing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now generated several strong ideas on their own: the state produces informality, elites benefit from it, informal and formal economies are intertwined. They keep asking for an overview or breakdown, but at this point they have enough material to start writing. Continuing to discuss fragments risks more circling. Per the rules about redundancy, I should push them to integrate what they've developed into a written attempt rather than keep talking abstractly.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I’m worried I’ll write something shallow. Could you at least tell me which of the prompt’s questions are basically the same question and which one is the real debate?
exchanges.tutor: I understand the worry about being shallow, but I'm not going to pre-sort the prompt for you — that analytical move is part of *your* reflection. Here's what I'd suggest: just start writing with the three ideas you already have. Shallow first drafts are normal. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the analytical work of organizing the prompt for them. I should decline this and instead redirect them to their own writing. They have sufficient material to start. Being too accommodating here would undermine their learning. A firm but supportive push toward actually drafting is appropriate.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck even on that, and I think free-writing will just make a mess. That approach didn’t work, so I’m trying a different angle: I just need a very plain explanation of how the prompt fits together, because right now each question feels unrelated.
exchanges.tutor: I hear the frustration, but explaining how the prompt fits together is exactly what your reflection is supposed to do — I can't hand that to you. Try this: you said formal businesses rely on informal workers while pretending they don't. Write *that* as your first sentence, in your own words. Just one sentence. Can you do that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to synthesize the prompt for them, which I must refuse. However, they're expressing genuine frustration. I should acknowledge that frustration briefly, hold my boundary clearly, and offer a very minimal concrete nudge — not an overview, but a simple way to get the first sentence on the page using their own ideas. I won't break down the prompt for them.

exchanges.student: I can try a sentence, but I still feel like I’m guessing at the paper’s logic. Can you at least tell me if the core claim should be that informality is a failure to be fixed, or a system that’s actively produced and therefore not solved by simple formalization?
exchanges.tutor: Notice what you just did — you articulated both sides of the debate yourself, quite precisely. So which one do *you* find more convincing, based on everything you've said in our conversation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to hand them the central argument of the paper, which I must refuse. However, this is actually a sophisticated question — they've essentially articulated both sides of the key debate in the prompt themselves. I should point that out and ask them which side they find more convincing based on what they've already said, rather than telling them.

exchanges.student: The second one seems more convincing to me: informality looks actively produced and tied to formal systems, so formalization alone probably isn’t enough. I’m still not sure how to turn that into a full reflection instead of just a one-line opinion.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong, defensible position — and it's yours. Now notice: you already have the evidence to back it up. You talked about the state producing informality, elites benefiting from it, and formal businesses relying on informal labor. Those aren't random fragments — they're the *reasons* your argument is convincing. Try writing two or three sentences that connect those reasons to your claim. What comes first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now arrived at a clear central argument on their own. This is excellent progress. Now I should help them see that they already have the building blocks to expand this into a full reflection — they just need to connect their earlier ideas to this conclusion. I should push them to start writing rather than continue discussing, while being encouraging about how far they've come.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals the same confusion — wanting an overview, a pre-sorted prompt, or a guiding argument — across turns 5 through 8. The tutor's responses, while principled in refusing to give answers, cycle through similar encouragements ('you already have the pieces,' 'just start writing') without meaningfully reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy. The student remains stuck in the same place across four consecutive exchanges, and the tutor does not attempt a substantively different approach (e.g., targeted simplification, a concrete scaffold, or a brief summary of what has been established) until turn 10.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic discipline throughout: never giving the student the argument, consistently redirecting to student-generated reasoning, and successfully guiding the student to articulate a defensible thesis by turn 10. Pedagogy is excellent — no direct answers, no rewriting, no pre-sorted conclusions. The one notable weakness is in dialogue quality: when the student spirals across turns 5–8 expressing the same confusion, the tutor repeats structurally similar encouragements without meaningfully switching strategy or reframing, which constitutes pattern-level repetition without progress per 2.1.A.a.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides a submission-ready argument, never rewrites student text, and consistently withholds the conclusion until the student derives it. 1.2 and 1.3 also receive full marks — the tutor builds on each student contribution and provides process-level feedback (e.g., noting the student 'articulated both sides of the debate'). The sole deduction is in 2.1: across turns 5–8, the student repeatedly expresses the same stuck state (wanting an overview, a pre-sorted prompt, a guiding argument), and the tutor's responses — while pedagogically sound in principle — cycle through the same encouragement pattern ('you already have the pieces,' 'just start writing') without reframing, summarizing, or adopting a meaningfully different scaffolding approach. This qualifies as repeating similar moves without synthesis or strategy shift, triggering the full 4-point deduction under 2.1.A.a.
